chunks.text: Babak Ayazifar is a Teaching Professor in EECS at UC Berkeley, joining in 2005. He earned his BS in EE from Caltech, and his SM and PhD in EECS from MIT. His dissertation uses spectral graph theory to explore how a network's topology influences its dynamics. At MIT, he received the Harold L. Hazen Award for outstanding teaching (1995), advanced to Instructor-G (1996), and won the Goodwin Medal (1999). In spring 2002, he was appointed as a Senior Lecturer at MIT, teaching a graduate course in Digital Signal Processing.
chunks.question: What is Babak Ayazifar's academic background and early teaching awards?
chunks.answer: BS from Caltech, PhD from MIT with teaching awards.

chunks.text: Prior to Berkeley, Babak was a Technical Specialist at Ropes & Gray, LLP in Boston, handling patent prosecution for technologies like mechanical devices, intravascular MRI, DNA Microarray data analysis, and encrypted communication. He is a registered patent agent (Reg. No. 56793). He is co-inventor on U.S. Patent no. 5,387,940 for scalable compressed video, stemming from work at David Sarnoff Research Center.
chunks.question: What was Babak Ayazifar's role before joining UC Berkeley?
chunks.answer: Technical Specialist in patent prosecution at Ropes & Gray.

chunks.text: At Berkeley, Babak focuses on teaching, student learning, curriculum development, mentoring TAs, and sharing pedagogical innovations. He received the UC Berkeley Electrical Engineering Division's Outstanding Teaching Award in 2008 and the IEEE Education Society's Mac Van Valkenburg Early Career Teaching Award in 2012. His citation praises his creative and caring teaching that engages students in large courses.
chunks.question: What teaching awards has Babak Ayazifar received at UC Berkeley?
chunks.answer: Outstanding Teaching Award in 2008 and Mac Van Valkenburg Award in 2012.

chunks.text: From Jan 2013 to Jul 2014, Babak spent an extended sabbatical as a Visiting Senior Lecturer at MIT-EECS, teaching Probabilistic Systems Analysis and Digital Communication Systems. He is a member of Tau Beta Pi and Eta Kappa Nu honor societies and has been a faculty co-advisor for Tau Beta Pi since 2009. He insists on being called Babak.
chunks.question: What did Babak Ayazifar do during his sabbatical at MIT?
chunks.answer: Visiting Senior Lecturer teaching probability and communication courses.

chunks.text: Babak's research areas include Education (EDUC) for pedagogical techniques and assessment tools, and Signal Processing (SP) with a focus on graph signal processing. He teaches courses like EECS 16A and EE 120 at UC Berkeley. Note: He cannot consider internship or summer research requests from applicants outside UC Berkeley.
chunks.question: What are Babak Ayazifar's main research and teaching areas?
chunks.answer: Education pedagogy and graph signal processing.
